anatomy.q: The morula to blastocyst transition involves:

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding cleavage patterns in mammals?

o.C: Holoblastic and rotational, with asynchronous divisions of blastomeres

anatomy.q: The zona pellucida functions to:

anatomy.q: Which of the following describes the inner cell mass at the late blastocyst stage?

anatomy.q: Cleavage of the zygote is characterized by:

anatomy.q: The trophoblast differentiates into:

o.A: Enable direct contact between trophoblast and maternal endometrium, facilitating implantation
o.D: Increase the size of the embryo

anatomy.q: Which of the following occurs first during blastocyst implantation?

o.C: Apposition of the blastocyst to the endometrial epithelium

o.B: It is part of the hypoblast only

anatomy.q: Cleavage divisions in the human zygote are described as:

o.B: Holoblastic, rotational, and asynchronous, resulting in progressively smaller blastomeres

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE about the zona pellucida?

anatomy.q: The hatching of the blastocyst occurs approximately:

o.B: At the end of the second week

anatomy.q: Which of the following statements about implantation is correct?

o.C: The inner cell mass implants first

o.A: Observing cleavage in the uterine tube
o.B: Presence of the inner cell mass in the blastocoel

anatomy.q: Which of the following occurs during the third week of intrauterine life?

anatomy.q: The notochordal process, which forms in the third week, is important because it:

o.B: Defines the axis of the embryo and induces the formation of the neural plate

anatomy.q: The extraembryonic coelom (chorionic cavity) forms within:

anatomy.q: Which statement about gastrulation is correct?

anatomy.q: The prechordal plate forms from:

o.A: Cranial end of the primitive streak, serving as an organizer for head structures

anatomy.q: Which of the following statements regarding the allantois is TRUE during the third week?

anatomy.q: The notochordal canal forms when:

o.D: The notochordal process fuses with the endoderm, establishing a temporary lumen

anatomy.q: The intraembryonic mesoderm spreads between:

o.A: Epiblast and hypoblast, giving rise to somites, notochord, and lateral plate mesoderm

anatomy.q: The embryonic disc at the end of the third week has:

o.D: Three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm, with beginning of axial orientation

anatomy.q: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the primitive streak?

o.A: Establishes bilateral symmetry, serves as the site for mesoderm formation, and guides gastrulation

anatomy.q: Which of the following events occurs during the second week of intrauterine life?

o.D: Formation of lacunar networks in the syncytiotrophoblast to initiate maternal-fetal circulation

anatomy.q: The epiblast is continuous with:
The notochord serves as a signaling center for:

anatomy.q: Extraembryonic mesoderm splits to form:

o.A: Chorionic cavity (extraembryonic coelom), surrounding the amniotic cavity and yolk sac

anatomy.q: The primary chorionic villi are formed by:

anatomy.q: Which of the following occurs at the end of the third week?

anatomy.q: The mesoderm differentiates into all EXCEPT:

anatomy.q: The oropharyngeal membrane forms at:

o.D: Cranial end of the embryonic disc, marking future mouth, consisting of ectoderm and endoderm without intervening mesoderm

anatomy.q: The cloacal membrane forms at:

o.C: Caudal end of the embryonic disc, marking future anus and urogenital openings

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE about the extraembryonic mesoderm?

o.B: Contributes to connecting stalk, chorionic plate, and formation of the chorionic cavity

anatomy.q: The early maternal-fetal circulation is established through:

o.B: Lacunae in the syncytiotrophoblast, forming intervillous spaces

anatomy.q: The extraembryonic mesoderm differentiates into:

o.C: Somatic and splanchnic layers, surrounding the amniotic cavity and yolk sac

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE about the amniotic cavity?

o.B: Forms within the epiblast and expands to surround the developing embryo, providing a protective fluid-filled space

anatomy.q: Which of the following structures appears first during the third week?
The hypoblast contributes to:

o.A: Formation of the primary yolk sac and extraembryonic endoderm

anatomy.q: During the second week, the syncytiotrophoblast invades:

o.A: Maternal endometrial tissue, forming lacunae that later fill with maternal blood

anatomy.q: The extraembryonic coelom separates the mesoderm into:

o.B: Somatic mesoderm (lining cytotrophoblast) and splanchnic mesoderm (lining yolk sac)

anatomy.q: Which of the following marks the beginning of gastrulation?

o.A: Formation of the primitive streak along the caudal midline of the epiblast

anatomy.q: The notochordal process induces:

o.A: Formation of neural plate in overlying ectoderm, initiating neurulation

anatomy.q: The connecting stalk is derived from:

o.D: Extraembryonic mesoderm, linking the embryo to the trophoblast and forming future umbilical cord

anatomy.q: Lacunar networks in the syncytiotrophoblast connect to:

o.A: Maternal sinusoids, forming early uteroplacental circulation

anatomy.q: The primary chorionic villi consist of:
Which statement is TRUE about the third week embryonic disc?

o.C: Trophoblast forms the three germ layers
o.D: It is trilaminar with ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm, with established cephalocaudal and lateral axes

anatomy.q: The hypoblast contributes to which extraembryonic structure?
The intraembryonic mesoderm forms by migration of epiblast cells through:
Which structure separates the intraembryonic coelom into somatic and splanchnic layers?

o.A: Are part of the extraembryonic mesoderm
o.B: Are bilaminar, consisting of ectoderm and endoderm without intervening mesoderm, and mark the future mouth and anus, respectively
o.C: Give rise to the notochord and somites
o.D: Form from epiblast only and contribute to the primitive streak

anatomy.q: During the third week, somites form from:

o.C: Paraxial mesoderm on either side of the notochord, giving rise to vertebrae, skeletal muscle, and dermis

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the formation of the neural plate in the third week?

anatomy.q: The embryonic period is defined as:

o.A: Weeks 3–8 of intrauterine life, during which organogenesis occurs and major body structures are established
o.B: Weeks 9–38, when growth is primarily in size
o.C: Weeks 1–2, consisting of fertilization and implantation

anatomy.q: The fetal period begins at:

o.A: Week 9, characterized by growth, further maturation of organs, and functional development

anatomy.q: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding somite development?

o.A: Somites appear along the paraxial mesoderm during the third week and give rise to vertebrae, skeletal muscle, and dermis
o.D: Somites appear during the fetal period only

anatomy.q: During the fourth week, the embryo undergoes:

o.A: Folding in both longitudinal and transverse planes, forming the basic body shape and bringing the gut tube inside

anatomy.q: Which of the following is a consequence of abnormal folding of the embryo?

o.A: Gastroschisis or omphalocele, where abdominal contents fail to be enclosed

anatomy.q: During the fifth week, limb buds appear as:

o.A: Small protrusions of mesenchyme covered by ectoderm, indicating future upper and lower limbs

o.D: Occurs only in the fetal period

anatomy.q: During the sixth week, the head is disproportionately large due to:

o.A: Rapid brain growth and development of pharyngeal arches

anatomy.q: The pharyngeal arches contribute to:

o.A: Face, neck, jaw, ear, and associated structures

anatomy.q: Which statement is TRUE about the development of the neural tube?

o.A: It forms from the neural plate during the third and fourth weeks and closes cranially and caudally by day 26 and 28, respectively
o.B: Forms during the fetal period only

anatomy.q: Which of the following is a clinical correlation of neural tube defects?

o.A: Anencephaly or spina bifida, resulting from failure of neural tube closure

anatomy.q: During the seventh week, the embryo shows:

o.A: Rotation of the limbs and beginning of chondrification centers in developing bones

anatomy.q: Chondrification centers:

o.A: Represent the initial cartilage models of future bones, appearing around week 7–8
o.B: Are sites of ossification in the fetal period only

anatomy.q: The liver begins hematopoiesis at approximately:

o.A: Week 6–7, serving as the major site of blood cell formation before the bone marrow takes over

anatomy.q: The fetal circulation is unique because:

o.A: Oxygenated blood from the placenta bypasses the liver via the ductus venosus and the lungs via the foramen ovale and ductus arteriosus
o.C: Oxygen comes primarily from maternal lungs

anatomy.q: The lungs in the fetus remain non-functional for gas exchange because:

o.A: Pulmonary circulation is largely bypassed and alveoli are filled with fluid
o.B: They do not develop until postnatal life

anatomy.q: The kidneys begin to develop from:

o.A: Intermediate mesoderm, forming pronephros, mesonephros, and metanephros sequentially

anatomy.q: The placenta is fully functional by:

o.A: End of the first trimester, facilitating maternal-fetal exchange of nutrients, gases, and waste

anatomy.q: The fetal period is characterized by:

o.A: Growth in size, maturation of organ systems, and development of functional capabilities

anatomy.q: During fetal development, ossification centers appear in:

o.A: Week 8–12, starting with primary centers in long bones and vertebrae

anatomy.q: Which statement is TRUE about the development of the external genitalia?

o.A: Differentiation begins around week 7, under the influence of sex chromosomes and hormones, with male and female structures initially indistinguishable
o.B: Differentiation occurs in the first week

anatomy.q: The primary ossification centers in long bones appear in:
Which of the following statements regarding fetal circulation is correct?

o.A: The foramen ovale allows oxygenated blood from the right atrium to enter the left atrium, bypassing non-functional lungs
o.B: Pulmonary artery carries only deoxygenated blood to lungs for oxygenation
o.D: Umbilical arteries carry oxygenated blood to the placenta

anatomy.q: The fetal liver is involved in:

o.A: Hematopoiesis from week 6 until bone marrow takes over in the fifth month

anatomy.q: The fetal kidneys begin to excrete urine into the amniotic fluid around:
The primary somites differentiate into:

o.A: Upper limbs rotating laterally and lower limbs medially, establishing final limb orientation

anatomy.q: The fetal adrenal cortex is derived from:

o.A: Mesoderm and produces steroid precursors for fetal development

anatomy.q: The fetal thyroid gland begins producing thyroxine by:

o.A: Week 12–14, essential for early metabolic regulation

anatomy.q: The fetal lungs are filled with fluid secreted by:

o.A: Pulmonary epithelium, maintaining alveolar expansion and development

anatomy.q: The ductus arteriosus connects:

o.A: Pulmonary artery to the aorta, bypassing non-functional lungs

anatomy.q: The ductus venosus allows:

o.A: Oxygenated blood from the umbilical vein to bypass the liver and enter the inferior vena cava
o.C: Blood to flow from right atrium to left atrium
o.D: Placental blood to enter left ventricle directly

anatomy.q: The fetal pancreas begins endocrine function by secreting insulin at approximately:
The fetal spleen serves primarily as:

o.A: A hematopoietic organ during the first trimester before bone marrow predominates

anatomy.q: The fetal gonads differentiate based on:

o.A: Sex chromosomes (XY for testis, XX for ovary) and SRY gene in males

anatomy.q: The fetal heart achieves four-chamber structure by:

o.A: Week 7–8, with septation of atria and ventricles

anatomy.q: The external genitalia become distinguishable around:
Fetal movements (quickening) are first perceived by the mother around:
Lanugo hair first appears:

o.A: Around week 20, covering fetal body and aiding in vernix caseosa adherence

anatomy.q: The fetal skin is initially:

o.A: Thin and transparent, gradually thickening and keratinizing toward the end of the second trimester
o.C: Identical to adult skin at week 8

anatomy.q: The fetal liver is the main site of:
The placenta acts as:

o.A: Endocrine organ secreting hCG, progesterone, and estrogens, and as an exchange organ between maternal and fetal blood

anatomy.q: The umbilical cord contains:

o.A: Two umbilical arteries and one umbilical vein, surrounded by Wharton’s jelly

anatomy.q: The fetal brain develops from:

o.A: Neural tube, forming forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain by week 5–6

anatomy.q: Primary ossification centers first appear in:
The vernix caseosa appears around:

o.A: Week 20, composed of desquamated epithelial cells and sebaceous secretions, protecting fetal skin

anatomy.q: Fetal swallowing begins around:

o.A: Week 12, contributing to amniotic fluid circulation and gastrointestinal maturation

anatomy.q: The fetal heart rate becomes detectable by ultrasound at:
The fetal liver, pancreas, and bone marrow coordinate to:

o.A: Support hematopoiesis, endocrine function, and growth, ensuring proper development of the fetus

anatomy.q: The amniotic cavity forms within the epiblast and:

o.B: Is lined by amnioblasts, gradually expanding to surround the embryo and providing a fluid-filled protective environment

anatomy.q: The amniotic fluid functions to:

o.A: Serve as a source of hematopoiesis
o.B: Cushion the fetus, allow movement, prevent adhesions, and maintain constant temperature

anatomy.q: The primary yolk sac develops from:

o.A: Hypoblast cells migrating along the inner surface of the blastocyst cavity, forming the exocoelomic membrane

anatomy.q: The secondary (definitive) yolk sac is formed by:

o.A: Pinching off of the primary yolk sac and is surrounded by extraembryonic mesoderm, serving as a site for early hematopoiesis and germ cell development

anatomy.q: The allantois appears around:

o.B: Day 16–17 as an endodermal diverticulum from the caudal wall of the yolk sac, later forming part of the umbilical vessels

anatomy.q: The allantois contributes to:

o.B: Formation of the umbilical arteries and veins, and early bladder development

anatomy.q: The umbilical cord is formed by:

o.B: The connecting stalk, which becomes vascularized with allantoic vessels and is surrounded by Wharton’s jelly for protection

anatomy.q: The umbilical cord typically contains:

o.B: Two umbilical arteries and one umbilical vein, surrounded by Wharton’s jelly

anatomy.q: The primary functions of the umbilical cord include:

o.A: Transport of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood between fetus and placenta, and structural support for fetal attachment

anatomy.q: The placenta develops from:

o.B: Chorion frondosum (villous trophoblast and extraembryonic mesoderm) and decidua basalis of the maternal endometrium

anatomy.q: The chorion consists of:

o.B: Cytotrophoblast and syncytiotrophoblast, plus underlying extraembryonic mesoderm

anatomy.q: The primary chorionic villi:

o.B: Are projections of cytotrophoblast covered by syncytiotrophoblast, appearing around day 13–14, and later develop into secondary and tertiary villi

anatomy.q: Secondary chorionic villi form when:

o.B: Extraembryonic mesoderm grows into the core of primary villi

anatomy.q: Tertiary chorionic villi are characterized by:

o.B: Development of fetal blood vessels within the mesodermal core, establishing fetoplacental circulation

anatomy.q: Maternal blood enters the intervillous spaces via:

o.B: Spiral arteries, allowing exchange of nutrients, gases, and waste with fetal capillaries

anatomy.q: Functions of the placenta include all EXCEPT:
The amniotic membrane is derived from:
The amniotic fluid is initially derived from:

o.B: Maternal plasma diffusing across amnion, later supplemented by fetal urine

anatomy.q: Functions of the yolk sac include:

o.B: Early hematopoiesis, germ cell origin, and formation of vitelline vessels

anatomy.q: The vitelline duct connects:

o.C: The yolk sac to the midgut of the embryo temporarily

anatomy.q: The allantois contributes to:

o.B: Umbilical vessels and formation of urinary bladder

anatomy.q: The umbilical vein carries:

o.A: Deoxygenated blood from fetus to placenta
o.B: Oxygenated blood from the placenta to the fetus

anatomy.q: The umbilical arteries carry:

o.B: Deoxygenated blood from the fetus to the placenta

anatomy.q: Wharton’s jelly:

o.B: Provides structural protection to the umbilical cord vessels and prevents compression

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE about the placenta?

o.B: It is hemochorial in humans, meaning maternal blood is in direct contact with the chorionic villi

anatomy.q: The intervillous space contains:

o.B: Maternal blood, bathing the fetal chorionic villi for exchange

anatomy.q: The placenta secretes:

o.B: hCG, progesterone, estrogens, and human placental lactogen

anatomy.q: Placental barrier functions include all EXCEPT:
Amniotic fluid volume peaks around:
The secondary yolk sac persists until:

o.B: End of the first trimester, after which it largely regresses

anatomy.q: The allantois contributes to the formation of:

o.B: Urachus, connecting the bladder to the umbilicus

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the amniotic membrane?

o.B: Forms a protective lining for the amniotic cavity, contributing to amniotic fluid containment and fetal protection

anatomy.q: The placental villi are covered by:

o.B: Syncytiotrophoblast, with underlying cytotrophoblast and mesoderm containing fetal capillaries

anatomy.q: Placental transfer of maternal antibodies occurs via:

o.B: IgG across the syncytiotrophoblast by receptor-mediated endocytosis

anatomy.q: The human placenta is:
The maternal component of the placenta is derived from:
The fetal component of the placenta is derived from:
The placenta reaches maximum thickness at:

o.B: Approximately 2–3 cm in late second trimester

anatomy.q: The amnion is attached to the:
The amniotic cavity expands rapidly during:

o.B: Second trimester, accommodating fetal growth and movement

anatomy.q: The umbilical cord is surrounded by:

o.B: Wharton’s jelly, derived from extraembryonic mesoderm

anatomy.q: The allantois forms:

o.B: Part of the urinary bladder and urachus

anatomy.q: Placental hormone hCG is secreted by:
The yolk sac is essential for:

o.B: Formation of primordial germ cells, early hematopoiesis, and early endodermal contribution

anatomy.q: Placental villi contain:
The chorionic cavity is also called:
Maternal-fetal exchange occurs across:

o.B: Thin syncytiotrophoblast layer and underlying cytotrophoblast and connective tissue of villi

anatomy.q: Amniotic fluid is swallowed by the fetus to:

o.B: Practice swallowing, aid gastrointestinal development, and regulate fluid balance

anatomy.q: The placenta allows selective passage of:

o.B: Nutrients, gases, and maternal antibodies while restricting most pathogens

anatomy.q: Abnormal development of umbilical cord, such as single umbilical artery, may lead to:

o.B: Congenital anomalies including cardiovascular, renal, or chromosomal abnormalities

anatomy.q: Teratology is defined as:

o.A: The study of normal organogenesis only
o.B: The study of congenital malformations and abnormal development of embryos and fetuses

anatomy.q: A teratogen is:

o.B: Any agent that can cause structural or functional abnormalities in a developing embryo or fetus

anatomy.q: Critical period of teratogenesis refers to:

o.B: Specific time frames during organogenesis when the developing organ is most sensitive to teratogenic agents

anatomy.q: Thalidomide exposure during pregnancy most commonly leads to:

o.A: Phocomelia, characterized by severely shortened or absent limbs

anatomy.q: Which of the following is TRUE about the effect of teratogens?

o.A: Teratogens have equal effect throughout gestation
o.B: The effect depends on dose, timing, genetic susceptibility, and the specific organ system developing at exposure
o.D: Effects are reversible in all cases

anatomy.q: The most sensitive period for neural tube defects is:

o.A: Weeks 3–4 of gestation, during closure of the neural tube

anatomy.q: Fetal alcohol syndrome is characterized by all EXCEPT:

o.A: Microcephaly, smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, and growth retardation

anatomy.q: Maternal diabetes can lead to:

o.A: Macrosomia, congenital heart defects, and caudal regression syndrome in the fetus

anatomy.q: TORCH infections in pregnancy are associated with:

o.C: Toxoplasmosis, Others (syphilis, varicella), Rubella, Cytomegalovirus, Herpes simplex virus causing congenital anomalies

anatomy.q: Congenital rubella syndrome can lead to:

o.A: Deafness, cataracts, cardiac defects, and microcephaly

anatomy.q: Epithelium is characterized by:

o.A: Widely spaced cells with abundant extracellular matrix
o.B: Lack of polarity and basement membrane
o.D: Closely packed cells with minimal intercellular space, polarity with apical and basal surfaces, and attachment to a basement membrane

anatomy.q: Simple squamous epithelium is specialized for:

o.C: Rapid diffusion, filtration, and lining of blood vessels (endothelium) and body cavities (mesothelium)

anatomy.q: Simple cuboidal epithelium is commonly found in:

o.B: Kidney tubules, thyroid follicles, and small ducts of glands, functioning in secretion and absorption

anatomy.q: Simple columnar epithelium may be:

o.C: Ciliated (uterine tube) or non-ciliated with microvilli (intestinal absorptive cells)

anatomy.q: Pseudostratified columnar epithelium is distinguished by:

o.B: All cells resting on the basement membrane but nuclei appearing at different levels, often ciliated in the respiratory tract

anatomy.q: Stratified squamous epithelium provides:

o.B: Protection against mechanical stress, abrasion, and dehydration; can be keratinized (skin) or non-keratinized (oral cavity)

anatomy.q: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) is specialized for:

o.B: Stretching and recoiling of the urinary tract, with cells changing shape from cuboidal to squamous when distended

anatomy.q: Microvilli are:

o.B: Apical cytoplasmic projections containing actin filaments that increase surface area for absorption, e.g., intestinal enterocytes

anatomy.q: Cilia are:

o.B: Motile apical projections containing microtubules (9+2 arrangement) and dynein arms, e.g., in respiratory epithelium and uterine tubes

anatomy.q: Stereocilia are:

o.C: Long, non-motile microvilli-like projections with actin filaments, found in epididymis and inner ear sensory cells

anatomy.q: Tight junctions (zonula occludens) function to:

o.A: Seal the intercellular space, prevent paracellular transport, and maintain cell polarity
o.C: Facilitate communication via ions and small molecules

anatomy.q: Adherens junctions (zonula adherens) are:

o.A: Actin filament-based junctions linking cells, providing mechanical stability and connecting to tight junctions apically

anatomy.q: Desmosomes (macula adherens) provide:

o.B: Strong intercellular adhesion by connecting intermediate filaments between adjacent cells, prominent in epidermis and cardiac muscle

anatomy.q: Hemidesmosomes:

o.B: Anchor epithelial cells to the basal lamina via integrins and intermediate filaments

anatomy.q: Gap junctions (nexus) consist of:

o.C: Connexons forming channels for ions and small molecules, enabling intercellular communication, e.g., cardiac and smooth muscle

anatomy.q: Basement membrane consists of:

o.C: Basal lamina (lamina lucida + lamina densa) and underlying reticular lamina, providing support, filtration, and attachment for epithelium

anatomy.q: Hemidesmosomes attach:

o.C: Intermediate filaments in basal cells to the basal lamina

anatomy.q: Junctional complex includes:

o.B: Tight junctions, adherens junctions, and sometimes desmosomes at the apical region of epithelial cells

anatomy.q: Epithelial polarity is maintained by:

o.B: Tight junctions separating apical and basolateral domains

anatomy.q: Simple epithelium is characterized by:

o.C: A single layer of cells in contact with the basement membrane

anatomy.q: Stratified epithelium is:

o.C: Multiple layers of cells with only the basal layer in contact with the basement membrane

anatomy.q: Ciliated columnar epithelium is found in:

o.D: Respiratory tract (trachea, bronchi) and uterine tubes, aiding in mucus clearance and ovum transport

anatomy.q: Keratinized stratified squamous epithelium:

o.C: Provides protection against dehydration and mechanical stress, e.g., epidermis

anatomy.q: Non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium is present in:

o.D: Oral cavity, esophagus, and vagina, remaining moist

anatomy.q: Goblet cells are:

o.C: Unicellular mucus-secreting cells interspersed among columnar epithelium of respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts

anatomy.q: Epithelium with microvilli has brush border seen in:

o.D: Small intestine and proximal renal tubules for absorption

anatomy.q: Zonula adherens links:

o.C: Actin cytoskeletons of adjacent epithelial cells near apical surface

anatomy.q: Macula adherens (desmosomes) are most abundant in:

o.C: Epidermis and cardiac intercalated discs for mechanical strength

anatomy.q: Gap junctions allow passage of:

o.B: Ions and small molecules up to ~1 kDa, enabling electrical and metabolic coupling

anatomy.q: Connexons are:

o.B: Hexameric assemblies of connexin proteins forming channels in gap junctions

anatomy.q: Hemidesmosomes attach to which type of filaments?
Tight junctions are located:

o.A: Apically between epithelial cells, preventing paracellular diffusion

anatomy.q: Basal lamina contains:

o.C: Type IV collagen, laminin, entactin, and heparan sulfate

anatomy.q: Pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium may contain:

o.B: Goblet cells secreting mucus, aiding mucociliary clearance

anatomy.q: Cilia motility depends on:

o.A: Dynein arms and ATP-driven microtubule sliding (9+2 arrangement)

anatomy.q: Zonula occludens prevent:

o.A: Movement of substances through the paracellular route

anatomy.q: Stratified cuboidal epithelium is found in:

o.C: Large ducts of sweat glands and mammary glands

anatomy.q: Stratified columnar epithelium is rare and found in:

o.A: Conjunctiva, male urethra, and large ducts of some glands

anatomy.q: Apical junctional complex order from apical to basal:
Epithelium regenerates from:

o.D: Basal layer stem cells continuously replacing lost cells

anatomy.q: Basal infoldings of epithelial cells are associated with:

o.D: Ion transport and increased surface area for Na+/K+ ATPase activity

anatomy.q: Epithelium covering the ovary (germinal epithelium) is:
Keratin filaments in epithelial cells are:

o.D: Intermediate filaments providing structural stability, particularly in desmosomes and hemidesmosomes

anatomy.q: The junctional complex is important for:

o.D: Maintaining tissue integrity, polarity, and selective permeability

anatomy.q: The apical surface specializations of epithelial cells include:

o.D: Microvilli, stereocilia, and cilia, depending on functional requirements

anatomy.q: Epithelium lacking blood vessels is termed:

o.D: Avascular, receiving nutrients via diffusion from underlying connective tissue

anatomy.q: Basement membrane disorders may lead to:

o.D: Epidermolysis bullosa due to defective anchoring of basal cells

anatomy.q: Gap junction dysfunction can result in:

o.D: Cardiac conduction defects due to impaired intercellular electrical coupling

anatomy.q: Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) involves:

o.D: Epithelial cells losing polarity and adhesion, gaining migratory properties during development and wound healing

anatomy.q: Claudins and occludins are integral proteins of:

o.D: Tight junctions, forming the paracellular barrier

anatomy.q: Adherens junctions are primarily linked to:
Desmosomal cadherins include:

o.D: Desmoglein and desmocollin connecting intermediate filaments of adjacent cells, providing mechanical strength

anatomy.q: Gap junctions are composed of:

o.D: Connexins forming connexons, allowing direct exchange of ions and small metabolites between cells

anatomy.q: Hemidesmosomes connect epithelial cells to:

o.D: Basal lamina via integrins and keratin filaments

anatomy.q: Tight junctions (zonula occludens) are located:

o.D: Apically between epithelial cells to prevent paracellular transport

anatomy.q: The primary function of microvilli is:

o.D: Increasing surface area for absorption, containing actin filaments as core

anatomy.q: Cilia are:

o.D: Motile apical projections with a 9+2 microtubule arrangement, responsible for moving mucus and fluids

anatomy.q: Stereocilia are found in:

o.D: Epididymis and inner ear hair cells; non-motile, actin-based projections for absorption and mechanosensation

anatomy.q: Claudins and occludins are integral proteins of:

o.D: Tight junctions forming the paracellular barrier

anatomy.q: Zonula adherens (adherens junctions) are located:

o.D: Just basal to tight junctions, linking actin cytoskeletons of neighboring cells for mechanical stability

anatomy.q: Connective tissue is characterized by:

o.A: Closely packed cells with minimal matrix
o.B: Lack of blood supply in all types
o.D: Abundant extracellular matrix, few scattered cells, and specialized fibers providing support, strength, and elasticity

anatomy.q: The main components of connective tissue matrix are:

o.D: Fibers (collagen, elastic, reticular) and ground substance (proteoglycans, glycosaminoglycans, glycoproteins)

anatomy.q: Fibroblasts are:

o.D: The principal connective tissue cells responsible for synthesis of collagen, elastic fibers, and ground substance

anatomy.q: Collagen fibers are composed of:

o.D: Type I collagen forming strong, rope-like bundles providing tensile strength

anatomy.q: Reticular fibers are composed of:

o.D: Type III collagen forming fine branching networks supporting hematopoietic and lymphoid organs

anatomy.q: Elastic fibers are composed of:

o.D: Elastin core surrounded by fibrillin microfibrils, allowing tissues to stretch and recoil, e.g., aorta, vocal cords

anatomy.q: Ground substance of connective tissue contains:

o.D: Glycosaminoglycans, proteoglycans, and glycoproteins, facilitating diffusion of nutrients and waste between cells and blood vessels

anatomy.q: Mesenchymal cells are:

o.D: Embryonic connective tissue cells capable of differentiating into fibroblasts, chondroblasts, osteoblasts, adipocytes, and endothelial cells

anatomy.q: Loose (areolar) connective tissue is:

o.D: Highly cellular, abundant ground substance, with loosely arranged fibers, found beneath epithelia and around vessels and nerves

anatomy.q: Dense connective tissue is:

o.D: Rich in collagen fibers, providing tensile strength; may be regular (tendons, ligaments) or irregular (dermis, organ capsules)

anatomy.q: Adipose tissue functions include:

o.B: Energy storage, insulation, cushioning, and endocrine activity (leptin secretion)

anatomy.q: White (unilocular) adipose tissue contains:

o.A: Large single lipid droplet displacing nucleus to periphery, predominant in adults

anatomy.q: Brown (multilocular) adipose tissue is specialized for:

o.A: Thermogenesis via abundant mitochondria containing uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1), prominent in neonates and hibernating animals

anatomy.q: Mast cells:

o.D: Contain metachromatic granules with histamine, heparin, and proteases; involved in allergic reactions and inflammation

anatomy.q: Plasma cells:

o.B: Derived from B lymphocytes; produce antibodies, abundant basophilic cytoplasm, eccentric nucleus with clock-face chromatin

anatomy.q: Macrophages:

o.B: Phagocytic cells derived from monocytes; participate in defense, antigen presentation, and tissue remodeling

anatomy.q: Connective tissue proper is divided into:

o.A: Loose and dense connective tissue, depending on fiber density and arrangement

anatomy.q: Mucous connective tissue (Wharton’s jelly) is found in:

o.A: Umbilical cord, rich in hyaluronic acid, sparse fibers, gelatinous matrix

anatomy.q: Connective tissue fibers are produced by:
Type I collagen is abundant in:

o.A: Dermis, tendons, ligaments, and bone, providing tensile strength

anatomy.q: Type II collagen is found in:

o.A: Hyaline and elastic cartilage, vitreous body of the eye

anatomy.q: Type III collagen forms:

o.D: Reticular fibers supporting lymphoid and hematopoietic tissues

anatomy.q: Type IV collagen forms:

o.A: Network in basal lamina of basement membrane

anatomy.q: Reticular connective tissue stains with:

o.A: Silver salts (argentaffin reaction) due to type III collagen

anatomy.q: Elastic fibers are abundant in:

o.A: Aorta, large arteries, vocal cords, ligamentum nuchae, lungs

anatomy.q: Ground substance is:

o.A: Amorphous, hydrated material filling spaces between fibers and cells; permits diffusion and resists compressive forces

anatomy.q: Connective tissue cells can be classified as:

o.A: Fixed cells (fibroblasts, adipocytes, mast cells) and wandering cells (plasma cells, macrophages, leukocytes)

anatomy.q: Connective tissue with minimal cells and densely packed collagen is:

o.A: Dense connective tissue, regular (tendon, ligament) or irregular (dermis)

anatomy.q: Fibrocytes differ from fibroblasts by:

o.A: Being less active, spindle-shaped, with elongated nucleus and fewer organelles

anatomy.q: Ground substance of connective tissue contains:

o.A: Hyaluronic acid, chondroitin sulfate, dermatan sulfate, keratan sulfate, and glycoproteins like fibronectin

anatomy.q: Loose connective tissue is most abundant in:

o.B: Subepithelial regions, surrounding vessels and nerves, providing flexibility and cushioning

anatomy.q: Dense irregular connective tissue is present in:

o.A: Dermis, capsules of organs, periosteum, providing strength in multiple directions

anatomy.q: Dense regular connective tissue is:

o.A: Collagen fibers arranged in parallel bundles to resist unidirectional tension, e.g., tendons and ligaments

anatomy.q: Cartilage is:

o.A: Avascular connective tissue with chondrocytes embedded in lacunae and ECM rich in collagen and proteoglycans

anatomy.q: Connective tissue proper functions include:

o.B: Mechanical support, tissue repair, defense, nutrient storage, and transport

anatomy.q: Connective tissue fibers provide:

o.B: Mechanical strength, elasticity, and structural support

anatomy.q: Type VII collagen forms:

o.C: Anchoring fibrils connecting basal lamina to underlying connective tissue in skin

anatomy.q: Connective tissue cells are embedded in:

o.B: Extracellular matrix, consisting of fibers and ground substance

anatomy.q: Elastic cartilage contains:

o.B: Chondrocytes in lacunae and abundant elastic fibers in matrix, e.g., external ear, epiglottis

anatomy.q: Reticular connective tissue provides:

o.B: Fine supportive framework in lymph nodes, spleen, and bone marrow

anatomy.q: Loose connective tissue is rich in:

o.B: Cells and ground substance, with sparse collagen and elastic fibers

anatomy.q: Dense regular connective tissue resists:

o.C: Tension along one axis due to parallel collagen bundles

anatomy.q: Dense irregular connective tissue resists:

o.A: Mechanical stress in multiple directions due to interwoven collagen bundles

anatomy.q: Fibroblasts in connective tissue are identified by:

o.C: Spindle-shaped cells with elongated nuclei, prominent RER for protein synthesis

anatomy.q: Connective tissue provides:

o.D: Structural support, medium for exchange, protection, and tissue repair

anatomy.q: Mast cells originate from:

o.B: Bone marrow-derived precursors; mature in connective tissue, involved in inflammation and allergy

anatomy.q: Collagen fibrils exhibit:

o.B: Periodic banding pattern (67 nm) visible under electron microscope, providing tensile strength

anatomy.q: Connective tissue cells that produce antibodies are:

o.B: Plasma cells derived from B lymphocytes

anatomy.q: Dense connective tissue is mostly composed of:

o.A: Collagen fibers with few cells, providing high tensile strength

anatomy.q: Connective tissue plays a role in:

o.A: Immune defense, tissue repair, structural support, and storage of nutrients

anatomy.q: Elastic cartilage is characterized by:

anatomy.q: Fibrocartilage is found in:

anatomy.q: The epiphyseal plate is:

anatomy.q: Osteocytes communicate through:

anatomy.q: Fibrocartilage differs from hyaline cartilage in that:

anatomy.q: Chondroblasts:

anatomy.q: The primary function of cartilage is:

anatomy.q: Appositional growth of cartilage occurs by:

anatomy.q: Interstitial growth of cartilage occurs by:

anatomy.q: The organic part of bone matrix provides:

anatomy.q: Osteoclasts resorb bone by:

anatomy.q: Volkmann’s canals:

anatomy.q: Flat bones form by:

anatomy.q: Osteoid is:

o.B: Unmineralized bone matrix secreted by osteoblasts

anatomy.q: Cartilage is avascular, so:

anatomy.q: Elastic cartilage is found in:

anatomy.q: Osteoclast activity is stimulated by:

anatomy.q: The Haversian system is:

anatomy.q: Osteoprogenitor cells:

anatomy.q: Rickets in children leads to:

o.A: Soft, deformed bones due to poor mineralization at growth plates

anatomy.q: Achondroplasia affects:

o.C: Endochondral ossification, resulting in short-limbed dwarfism

anatomy.q: Osteoporosis is characterized by:

anatomy.q: Osteopetrosis is caused by:

o.A: Defective osteoclast function leading to abnormally dense but brittle bones

anatomy.q: Paget’s disease of bone involves:

anatomy.q: The epiphyseal plate closes due to:

anatomy.q: Osteogenesis imperfecta results from:

o.A: Defective mineral deposition in bone only
